title: Blog 1 - Credit Card Fraud Detection with Machine Learning on AWS
content:
Building a Real-Time Credit Card Fraud Detection System on AWS 1. Problem statement In the digital era, credit card fraud causes significant losses for the financial industry. The challenge is not only detecting fraudulent transactions, but also responding quickly: the system needs to identify abnormal signals almost immediately, before a transaction is completed or causes greater damage.
In this article, our team shares the process of designing a pipeline on AWS to automatically receive transactions, process data in real time, call a Machine Learning model to predict fraud risk, and send alerts to administrators when suspicious transactions are detected.
2. System architecture analysis The system is divided into two main areas: Training Zone and Real-time Zone. These two areas operate independently at different stages, but are connected through the model trained and deployed on Amazon SageMaker.
A. Training Zone Amazon S3 Data Lake serves as the storage location for historical transaction data. This data may include raw data, processed data, train/test datasets, and artifacts generated during training.
Amazon SageMaker is the central component of the training area. Data from S3 is loaded into SageMaker for preprocessing, feature engineering, model training, and evaluation. After the model achieves the expected results, artifacts such as model.joblib, scaler.joblib, and inference.py are packaged into model.tar.gz, then deployed as a SageMaker Real-time Endpoint to be ready for prediction.
B. Real-time Zone Amazon API Gateway and AWS Lambda are the entry points of the real-time system. When a user or transaction system sends a request, API Gateway receives the request and triggers Lambda to parse JSON, validate the data, and standardize the input format.
Amazon Kinesis Data Streams acts as a high-speed buffer for the transaction stream. Instead of having the input Lambda call the model directly, transactions are sent to Kinesis to decouple the data ingestion step from the prediction processing step. This design helps the pipeline handle load better when traffic spikes.
During inference, another Lambda reads data from Kinesis, performs feature mapping, and calls the SageMaker Endpoint to receive prediction results. If a transaction is classified as Fraud, the system sends an alert through Amazon SNS to the administrator’s email.
In parallel with the alerting process, transaction data and prediction results are written back to Amazon S3 through Kinesis Data Firehose. This data can be used for auditing, post-deployment analysis, or model retraining in the future.
3. Lessons and insights from the team During the architecture design process, the team drew several practical lessons about choosing and combining AWS services for the fraud detection problem.
Use services that match each specific role Initially, the team considered using a single Lambda to receive requests, process data, and call the model. This approach is simpler, but it can make the system tightly coupled between transaction ingestion and prediction.
Placing Amazon Kinesis in the middle helps separate these two parts. The input Lambda only needs to focus on receiving and validating data, while the downstream processing Lambda can read from the stream and call the SageMaker Endpoint. This design makes the system easier to scale and reduces the risk of losing transactions when traffic spikes.
Automate the alerting flow Instead of building an email server or integrating a third-party email API, the team uses Amazon SNS to send alerts. When the model predicts a transaction as fraudulent, SNS can send an email to the administrator almost immediately. Within the workshop scope, SNS is a suitable choice because it is simple to configure, easy to test, and integrates well with other AWS services.
Take advantage of SageMaker Real-time Endpoint One advantage of using SageMaker is that the process of moving a model from the training stage to the inference serving stage is simplified. After the model is packaged with the correct structure, SageMaker can deploy it as an endpoint that other components can call through an API. This helps the team focus more on the data flow and fraud detection logic instead of managing a model-serving server manually.
Reduce operational code with Kinesis Data Firehose If the team wrote custom code to collect streaming data and store it in S3, the system would need additional logic for batching, retries, and write failures. With Kinesis Data Firehose, prediction data can be automatically delivered to S3 without managing a separate server. This fits the goal of building a serverless pipeline that is easy to operate and scale.
4. Cost optimization strategy Cloud cost is a factor that needs to be considered from the design stage. For this problem, the team focuses on cost optimization in three main directions.
Prioritize serverless architecture Components such as API Gateway, Lambda, SNS, and Kinesis reduce the need to maintain continuously running EC2 servers. The system only incurs costs based on actual usage, making it suitable for demo environments, workshops, or early experimentation stages.
Optimize training cost with SageMaker Model training can consume significant CPU/GPU resources. With SageMaker, the team can consider using Managed Spot Training to take advantage of AWS spare instances, thereby reducing training costs compared with always using On-Demand Instances.
Manage the data lifecycle on S3 Transaction data and prediction history may grow over time. Therefore, the team proposes using S3 Lifecycle Policies to automatically move older data to lower-cost storage classes such as S3 Standard-IA or S3 Glacier after a certain period. This approach helps retain data for long-term analysis while still controlling storage costs.
5. Future development directions Within the workshop scope, the system focuses on simulating an end-to-end pipeline and using a traditional Machine Learning model such as Random Forest to classify transactions as Fraud or Normal.
In the next development stages, the team can improve the AI core in SageMaker. Because financial fraud data is often highly imbalanced, data balancing techniques such as SMOTE-ENN can be tested to improve fraud-class detection. In addition, deep learning models for time-series data such as Bi-LSTM can also be explored if the system needs to capture sequential transaction behavior.
Overall, this architecture demonstrates how AWS services such as S3, SageMaker, API Gateway, Lambda, Kinesis, Firehose, and SNS can be combined to build a real-time fraud detection pipeline. It provides a solid foundation for expanding into a more complete transaction monitoring system in the future.

title: Create the S3 bucket and Data Lake structure
content:
The first step is to create an S3 bucket to store all data and artifacts for the Fraud Detection system.
1. Open the Amazon S3 Console Access the AWS Console, search for the S3 service, and open the bucket management page.
2. Create a bucket for the project Choose Create bucket and name the bucket using an easy-to-recognize convention, for example:
fraud-detection-<your-name>-<date> The bucket name must be globally unique across AWS, so you can add your personal name or the creation date at the end of the bucket name.
Recommended configuration:
Configuration Recommended value Region us-east-1 Object Ownership ACLs disabled Block Public Access Enable all settings Bucket Versioning Can be disabled for the demo Default encryption Keep the default or enable SSE-S3 Do not make the bucket public because transaction data and model artifacts should not be publicly accessible.
3. Create the folder structure in the bucket After the bucket is created, create the following prefixes to make data management easier:
raw/ data_train/ model/ model.tar.gz Meaning:
raw/: contains the original creditcard.csv dataset file. data_train/: contains training data after the data preparation step. model/: contains model artifacts and related files. model.tar.gz: packaged model file used to create the SageMaker Endpoint. Expected result After this step, the S3 bucket is ready for uploading the dataset directly using the Amazon S3 Console and for storing artifacts from the Machine Learning pipeline.

title: Event 1 - FCAJ Community Day - Conference Call
content:
1. Event Information Event: FCAJ Community Day - Conference Call Date: Saturday, 23 May Venue: Bitexco Financial Tower Role: Participant 2. Evidence of Participation 3. Event Overview The FCAJ Community Day - Conference Call gathered technology enthusiasts, students, and industry professionals to share practical knowledge about Artificial Intelligence, AWS cloud services, large language models, and enterprise software development. Throughout the event, speakers introduced real-world experiences, technical insights, and product development stories that demonstrated how modern technologies are applied in business environments.
09:00 - 09:30 AM: Context Is Everything – Making AI Actually Work for You The opening presentation emphasized that providing sufficient context is essential for obtaining accurate and useful responses from AI systems. The speaker explained how AI performance depends on contextual information rather than prompts alone and introduced the concept of building a Second AI Brain through memory and structured knowledge. Practical suggestions were also provided to help learners interact with AI more effectively during both study and software development.
09:30 - 10:00 AM: 36 Hours with LotusHacks – Building UTMorpho from Idea to Reality This session shared the complete journey of a hackathon team that created UTMorpho within only 36 hours. The speakers described how the initial idea was formed, how user problems were identified, and how the solution evolved under strict time constraints. They also discussed technical challenges, teamwork experiences, and lessons learned throughout the competition before presenting a demonstration of the final product.
10:00 - 10:40 AM: From Edge to Origin – CloudFront as Your Foundation The presentation introduced Amazon CloudFront as an important component for building scalable and secure cloud applications. Topics included content delivery optimization, reducing operational costs, improving application security, increasing service reliability, and delivering faster responses to users located in different geographic regions.
10:40 - 10:55 AM: Friendly AI Assistant with Amazon Quick This session demonstrated several AI-powered tools within the Amazon Quick ecosystem that simplify data analysis and collaboration. The speaker introduced:
Quick Chat Agent for conversational data analysis. Quick Flows for creating intelligent workflows using natural language. Quick Spaces for sharing organizational knowledge. Quick Sight for generating reports and dashboards from raw data with AI assistance. 10:55 - 11:00 AM: Break Participants had a short networking break before the final technical sessions.
11:00 - 11:30 AM: Non-Determinism of “Deterministic” LLM Settings This presentation explained why Large Language Models may still generate different outputs even when configured with deterministic settings such as temperature = 0. The speaker discussed how token prediction works, the influence of inference optimizations, and why identical inputs do not always guarantee identical outputs. Several practical techniques for reducing inconsistency when deploying AI systems were also presented.
11:30 AM - 12:00 PM: Enterprise-Grade Multi-Agent System – Startup Credit Scoring Case Study The final session focused on designing enterprise-level multi-agent systems through a startup credit scoring scenario. The presentation explained the limitations of traditional banking evaluation methods when dealing with startup businesses and demonstrated how multiple AI agents can collaborate as a Virtual Credit Committee. Additional topics included system architecture, governance, compliance, deployment strategy, and long-term business value.
4. Reflection and Learning Outcomes Participating in this event provided valuable insights that closely relate to my internship project and future career development.
One of the most valuable lessons came from the presentation about AI context engineering. It highlighted that successful AI applications rely not only on advanced models but also on well-structured contextual information. This idea directly applies to my AWS-based fraud detection project, where carefully designed transaction features and preprocessing pipelines are essential for producing reliable predictions.
The session introducing Amazon CloudFront broadened my understanding of cloud architecture by demonstrating how performance, scalability, and security can be improved through content delivery optimization. Although CloudFront is not directly used in my current project, these concepts are highly relevant for deploying APIs and user-facing services in production environments.
Another important takeaway was the discussion about LLM non-determinism. Understanding that AI systems may still produce different outputs under seemingly identical settings emphasized the importance of evaluation, logging, monitoring, and guardrail mechanisms, particularly in financial applications where consistency and reliability are critical.
Finally, the presentation on multi-agent systems for credit scoring offered a broader perspective on applying AI in financial services. Beyond prediction accuracy, enterprise AI solutions must also provide transparency, compliance, and explainability. These ideas will be useful references when extending the architecture and future capabilities of my AWS Fraud Detection system.
As a participant, I attended every technical session, documented the key ideas presented by each speaker, and reflected on how these concepts relate to my internship project. The knowledge gained from this event will support the improvement of my internship report, especially in the areas of cloud architecture, AI system reliability, and future solution design.

title: Prepare the model training environment
content:
This section prepares the environment for data processing and Machine Learning model training.
You can do this in one of two ways:
Use Amazon SageMaker Notebook / Studio. Prepare the code on a local machine and then upload the required files to SageMaker/S3 through the AWS Console interface if needed. In this workshop, the preferred approach is to use SageMaker Notebook/Studio so that data, models, and AWS access permissions are managed more conveniently.
Required libraries pandas numpy scikit-learn joblib boto3 Check the Python environment You can use the following code snippet to check the main libraries:
import pandas as pd import numpy as np import sklearn import joblib import boto3 print("Environment is ready") Required input The dataset was uploaded to S3 using the Amazon S3 Console in section 5.3, for example:
s3://<bucket-name>/raw/creditcard.csv This path will be used as the input for the preprocessing and training step.

title: Worklog
content:
This section summarizes the 12-week internship process, with the main focus on building a real-time transaction fraud detection system using Machine Learning on AWS.
Throughout the implementation, the work was divided into several stages: learning about AWS, analyzing the fraud detection problem, preparing data, training a Machine Learning model, deploying the model on Amazon SageMaker, building a real-time pipeline with API Gateway, Lambda, Kinesis, SNS, Kinesis Firehose, and storing results on Amazon S3.
12-week overview Week 1: Getting familiar with AWS, the internship program, and project orientation
Week 2: Researching the fraud detection problem and system requirements
Week 3: Designing the overall architecture and Data Lake on Amazon S3
Week 4: Preprocessing data and building features for the model
Week 5: Training a Random Forest model for fraud detection
Week 6: Packaging the model and preparing the inference script
Week 7: Deploying a SageMaker Real-time Endpoint
Week 8: Building a transaction intake API with API Gateway and Lambda
Week 9: Integrating Kinesis and Lambda for real-time feature processing
Week 10: Processing prediction results, SNS alerts, and history storage with Firehose
Week 11: Monitoring, basic security, and cost optimization
Week 12: End-to-end testing, report finalization, and clean-up

title: Workshop overview
content:
1. Background Credit card fraud is an important problem in electronic payment systems. If a fraudulent transaction is not detected in time, it can cause financial loss, affect an organization’s reputation, and reduce user trust in the system.
In practice, fraud detection systems often need to process transactions almost in real time. When a new transaction appears, the system must quickly assess the risk level, determine whether the transaction is normal or suspicious, and then send an alert to the administrator if needed.
This workshop simulates such a system using Machine Learning and AWS services.
2. Workshop objectives In this workshop, we will build a real-time credit card fraud detection system using Machine Learning on AWS.
The system needs to achieve the following objectives:
Store historical transaction data on Amazon S3. Train a Machine Learning model with Amazon SageMaker. Use a Random Forest model to classify transactions as Fraud or Normal. Package the model and deploy it as a SageMaker Real-time Endpoint. Receive new transactions through Amazon API Gateway. Process realtime transactions with AWS Lambda and Amazon Kinesis. Call the SageMaker Endpoint to predict results. Send alerts through Amazon SNS if a fraudulent transaction is detected. Store prediction history to Amazon S3 through Kinesis Firehose. Monitor logs and metrics with Amazon CloudWatch. 3. Results after completion After completing the workshop, you will have a pipeline that includes:
An S3 Data Lake to store training data, model artifact, and prediction history. A Machine Learning model trained with SageMaker. A SageMaker Real-time Endpoint for realtime inference. An API endpoint for sending sample transactions. A Kinesis Data Stream to ingest realtime transactions. A Lambda Read Features function to process features and call the model. An SNS Topic to send email alerts when Fraud is detected. A Firehose Delivery Stream to write prediction history to S3. CloudWatch Logs to inspect the processing flow and debug errors. 4. Workshop architecture The architecture is divided into two processing zones.
4.1. Training Zone The Training Zone is responsible for preparing data, training the model, and deploying the model endpoint.
Amazon S3 │ ▼ Amazon SageMaker Training │ ▼ model.tar.gz │ ▼ SageMaker Real-time Endpoint In this zone:
Historical transaction data is stored on Amazon S3. SageMaker reads data from S3 to perform preprocessing, feature engineering, training, and model evaluation. After training, the Random Forest model is saved as model.joblib. If there is a data normalization step, the scaler is saved as scaler.joblib. The inference.py file defines how the model receives input and returns predictions. The files are packaged into model.tar.gz. SageMaker deploys the model as a Real-time Endpoint. 4.2. Real-time Zone The Real-time Zone is responsible for receiving new transactions, calling the model for prediction, sending alerts, and storing history.
User / Banking System │ ▼ Amazon API Gateway │ ▼ AWS Lambda │ ▼ Amazon Kinesis │ ▼ Lambda Read Features │ ▼ SageMaker Endpoint ├── Fraud ─► Amazon SNS ─► Email Admin └── Prediction Result │ ▼ Kinesis Firehose │ ▼ Amazon S3 In this zone:
A User or Banking System sends a transaction request through API Gateway. The input Lambda parses JSON, validates data, and normalizes the request. A valid transaction is put into Amazon Kinesis. Lambda Read Features reads records from Kinesis, performs feature engineering, and maps features. Lambda calls the SageMaker Endpoint to predict Fraud or Normal. If the result is Fraud, the system sends an alert through SNS to the Admin Email. The full prediction result is written to S3 through Kinesis Firehose. 5. Expected input and output data An input transaction may look like this:
{ "transaction_id": "txn-001", "user_id": "user-001", "transaction_amount": 2500000, "transaction_type": "online_payment", "merchant_category": "electronics", "location": "Ho Chi Minh City", "device_type": "mobile", "transaction_hour": 23 } The result returned by the model may be:
{ "prediction": "Fraud", "fraud_probability": 0.91 } Or:
{ "prediction": "Normal", "fraud_probability": 0.08 } 6. Important notes Do not use real credit card data in the workshop. Use only sample data or anonymized data for learning purposes. Do not hard-code AWS Access Key or Secret Key in source code. Use IAM Roles according to the least privilege principle. SageMaker Endpoint can incur costs while running, so it must be cleaned up after the demo. Each deployment step should be tested separately before running end-to-end.

title: Week 2 Worklog
content:
Time: 26/04/2026 - 02/05/2026
Week 2 Objectives Analyze the transaction fraud detection problem in the context of financial/banking systems. Define the system input, output, and success evaluation criteria. Choose a Machine Learning approach suitable for the scope of the internship project. Work completed Studied the characteristics of the fraud detection problem: Transaction data contains many attributes such as amount, time, transaction type, location, device, and behavior history. Fraud data is usually imbalanced compared with normal transactions. The system needs to respond quickly to support real-time alerts. Defined the project objectives: Receive transactions from a User or Banking System through API Gateway. Predict whether a transaction is Fraud or Normal. Return the fraud probability fraud_probability when available. Send alerts through SNS to the Admin Email when a suspicious transaction is detected.
